title: Machine-Learning Optimization and Characterization of a High-Optical-Depth Two-Color Nanofiber Trap
authors: W. Crump, M. Sadeghi, M. D. Hoogerland
abstract: Optical nanofibers provide a way of coupling quantum information in cold atoms across large distances, however, this coupling requires atoms to reside close to the nanofiber surface. Atoms can be trapped close to the surface using a two-color dipole trap. Here we present our experimental realization of a two-color dipole trap. We optimize the number of trapped atoms using a machine learning algorithm and measure the optical density via the transmission. We estimate the number of atoms in the trap to be approximately 1400 and the lifetime of the atoms in the trap to be 28 ms. Machine-learning optimization improved the on-resonance optical depth from 0.5 in the initial optimization stage to optical depths exceeding 15.
summary: The authors report an experimental realization of a two-color dipole trap using optical nanofibers to confine cold atoms near the fiber surface. They utilized machine learning to optimize the trapping process, achieving high optical densities and estimating $\sim 1400$ trapped atoms. The key finding is that while strong coupling is achieved, the measured lifetime is limited by technical noise rather than fundamental quantum processes.
main_problem: The goal was to create a robust, high optical-depth nanofiber trap for coupling quantum information from cold atoms over long distances.
main_result: The experiment successfully achieved an optical depth exceeding 15 and trapped approximately 1400 Cesium atoms with a measurable lifetime of 28 ms. The study concluded that technical noise, rather than fundamental processes, limits the atom loss rate.
method: The researchers employed machine learning optimization on the cooling sequence parameters and used transmission spectroscopy to characterize the optical depth and trap lifetime.
model_or_system: The physical system is a two-color evanescent-field dipole trap using Cesium atoms near an optical nanofiber. The trapping mechanism combines blue-detuned (repulsive) and red-detuned (attractive) fields, creating a 1D lattice of trapping sites.
key_observables: Peak optical depth ($\approx 15.1$), estimated atom number ($\approx 1400$), collective cooperativity ($\approx 28$), and trap lifetime ($28 	ext{ ms}$).
important_parameters: Trap depths ($\sim 100 \mu	ext{K}$), cooling detunings (up to $22 	ext{ MHz}$), and the characteristic loss rate dominated by technical noise.
main_assumptions: The primary limitation on lifetime is attributed to technical noise, not intrinsic recoil or vacuum processes. The calculation of single-atom optical depth might overestimate the true value due to wavefunction spreading.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the laser configuration and calculated potential energy landscapes. Figure 4 shows transmission measurements on resonance (left) and the exponential decay of optical depth versus absorbed probe photons (right).
paper_structure: The paper details the setup using a two-color dipole trap, describes the use of machine learning to optimize the cooling sequence parameters, presents characterization results like high optical depth measurement, and finally analyzes the lifetime decay to determine the limiting loss mechanism.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it pushes the boundaries of atom-photon interfaces by demonstrating high coupling strengths (high OD) in a shallow trap regime. The combination of ML optimization with precise quantum characterization makes it a model for advanced open quantum system experiments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Top) Configuration of dipole trap lasers through the optical nanofiber as well as measurement probe with the SPCM. (Bottom Left) Contributions to the potential energy from 685 nm laser (blue), 937 nm lasers (red), Van der Waals potential (green) and their sum (black) for a slice at y = 0 and z = 0 along the x direction showing a trap depth of roughly 105 uK at a distance of around 350 nm from the fiber surface. A slice along the y direction is also shown (grey). (Bottom Middle) Potential energy landscape in the x-y plane calcu- lated using powers and polarizations from the experiment. (Bottom Right) Potential energy landscape in the x-z plane calculated using powers and...

FIG. 2. (Top) Cooling sequence used in the experiment. The dots show values which where adjusted by the machine learning algo- rithm, as were the compression ramp time t1 and the PGC cooling time t2. (Bottom Left) Cost vs run number for an example machine learning session. The cost values are distinguished by whether the test parameters used were produced by the differential evolution al- gorithm or the neural net model. (Bottom Right) Cost vs Control voltage for three parameters varied during a machine learning ses- sion. These were the X, Y and Z Helmholtz magnetic fields at the beginning of the compression cooling step. Darker colors indicate values that were produced by the neural net...

FIG. 3. (Left) Transmission measurement through the fiber with atoms in the two-color trap. The fit is using equation 1 for the opti- cal density. (Right) Lifetime measurement of the atoms in the trap. Derived from optical density measurements for a range of times after turning off the cooling lasers.

FIG. 4. (Left) Transmission measurement on resonance through the fiber. (Right) Fitted optical density vs number of photons into the trap. The fit is exponential and decay constant is an average number of photons 2120 ± 60.
